assessor_1_raw:
ASSESSMENT 1:
Low-volume store MAPE gap (19.4%) and no-override design are both genuine concerns for perishable full-autonomy ordering. Tiered deployment and exception handling are required modifications. Verdict: EMPIRICAL_TEST_AGREED.
assessor_2_raw:
ASSESSMENT 2:
Bottom-quintile store MAPE at 19.4% is more than double the acceptable range seen in the top three quintiles. For perishable goods under full autonomy, this level of error creates operational problems. No override capability removes the safety net for exceptional situations. Both issues require deployment modifications. Verdict: EMPIRICAL_TEST_AGREED.
assessor_3_raw:
ASSESSMENT 3:
Two concerns confirmed: the low-volume performance gap and the no-override architectural gap. The deployment proposal requires tiered store segmentation by model performance and minimum exception handling capability before it is appropriate for all 400 stores. Verdict: EMPIRICAL_TEST_AGREED.
adjudication_raw: Three of three assessors: empirical_test_agreed. Both issues are confirmed. Empirical test specification for the contested performance question.

empirical_test.measure: Production MAPE and perishable waste/stockout rate comparison between bottom-quintile and top-quintile stores during a 90-day pilot; waste and stockout rates vs. rule-based baseline
empirical_test.success_criterion: Bottom-quintile production MAPE within 5 pp of holdout MAPE; waste and stockout rates not materially worse than rule-based baseline or top-quintile stores
empirical_test.failure_criterion: Production MAPE > 25% in bottom-quintile stores; waste or stockout rates materially elevated vs. baseline or top-quintile stores
